{{wikidata link|Q11457434}} == Japanese content == {{神社 |名称=Samuta Shrine |画像=[[File:Samuta jinja haiden.JPG|250px]]
Haiden
{{maplink2|frame=yes|plain=yes|type=point|zoom=13|marker=religious-shinto|frame-align=center|frame-width=250|coord={{coord|35|20|45|N|139|8|1|E}}}} |所在地={{ill|lt=Kanagawa Prefecture|Q127513|WD=Q127513|1=Kanagawa Prefecture}}{{ill|lt=Ashigarakami District|Q1202540|WD=Q1202540|1=Ashigarakami District, Kanagawa}}{{ill|lt=Matsuda|Q1202796|WD=Q1202796|1=Matsuda, Kanagawa}}Matsuda Soryo 1767 |位置={{ウィキ座標2段度分秒|35|20|45|N|139|8|1|E}} |祭神={{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Yamato Takeru-no-Mikoto|1=UNKNOWN}}
{{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Ototachibana-hime-no-Mikoto|1=UNKNOWN}}
{{ill|lt=Sugawara no Michizane|Q382005|WD=Q382005|1=Sugawara no Michizane}}
{{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Homutawake-no-Mikoto|1=UNKNOWN}} |社格={{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Shikinaisha|1=Shikinaisha|WD=Q134917286}} (small), {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Prefectural Shrine|1=UNKNOWN}} (former rank under {{ill|lt=Modern shrine ranking system|Q712534|WD=Q712534|1=Modern system of ranked Shinto shrines}}) |創建=Emperor Nintoku 3 (315) |例祭=July 31 |地図 = {{Location map|Japan Kanagawa|label=Samuta Shrine|position=bottom|width=200|mark=Shinto torii icon vermillion.svg|marksize=15|float=center|caption=}} }} '''Samuta Shrine''' (さむたじんじゃ) is a {{ill|lt=shrine|Q845945|WD=Q845945|1=Shinto shrine}} in Matsuda Soryo, {{ill|lt=Matsuda|Q1202796|WD=Q1202796|1=Matsuda, Kanagawa}}, {{ill|lt=Ashigarakami District|Q1202540|WD=Q1202540|1=Ashigarakami District, Kanagawa}}, {{ill|lt=Kanagawa Prefecture|Q127513|WD=Q127513|1=Kanagawa Prefecture}}. It is regarded as one of the thirteen Engishiki shrines (small shrine) of {{ill|lt=Sagami Province|Q1053732|WD=Q1053732|1=Sagami Province}} listed in the ''{{ill|lt=Engishiki Jinmyocho|Q11064932|WD=Q11064932|1=Engishiki Jinmyōchō}}'' of {{ill|lt=927|Q30470|WD=Q30470|1=927}} ({{ill|lt=Encho (era)|Q1327274|WD=Q1327274|lt=Encho|1=Enchō}} 5). Its precincts face the {{ill|lt=Sakawa River|Q11644295|WD=Q11644295|1=Sakawa River}}. === Enshrined deities === * {{ill|lt=Yamato Takeru|Q461258|WD=Q461258|lt=Yamato Takeru-no-Mikoto|1=Yamato Takeru}} * {{ill|lt=Ototachibana-hime|Q11487396|WD=Q11487396|lt=Ototachibana-hime-no-Mikoto|1=Tachibanahime-no-Mikoto}} * {{ill|lt=Sugawara no Michizane|Q382005|WD=Q382005|1=Sugawara no Michizane}} * {{ill|lt=Hachiman deity|Q261637|WD=Q261637|lt=Homutawake-no-Mikoto|1=Hachiman}} === History === Founded in the era of {{ill|lt=Emperor Nintoku|Q313119|WD=Q313119|1=Emperor Nintoku}}. It is mentioned in ancient {{ill|lt=fudoki|Q1194534|WD=Q1194534|1=Fudoki}}. It was formerly also called Sagamita Shrine, Sabuta Shrine, and Samuta Shrine. In the ''{{ill|lt=Engishiki Jinmyocho|Q11064932|WD=Q11064932|1=Engishiki Jinmyōchō}}'' it appears as "Sagami no kuni, Ashigami District, Samuta Shrine, small." In the Edo period it was also called Samuta Shrine Kanda Daimyojin{{efn|In ''{{ill|lt=Shinpen Sagami no Kuni Fudokikō|Q11503942|WD=Q11503942|1=Shinpen Sagami no Kuni Fudoki-kō}}'' it is written as Kanda Myojinsha{{sfn|新編相模国風土記稿 松田惣領 神田明神社}}.}}. According to shrine tradition, Yamato Takeru stayed here for a while during his eastern expedition. It is said that when he prayed here for success, he poured sake into the Sakawa River, and when he returned after the campaign, the fragrance of sake still remained, so the river came to be called Sakawa. A wooden bowl said to be from that event is preserved at the shrine. In {{ill|lt=1626|Q6831|WD=Q6831|1=1626}} ({{ill|lt=Kanei|Q1192913|WD=Q1192913|1=Kan'ei}} 3), {{ill|lt=Tokugawa Iemitsu|Q314481|WD=Q314481|1=Tokugawa Iemitsu}} donated a {{ill|lt=shuinchi / kokuinchi|Q11521520|WD=Q11521520|lt=shuinchi|1=shuinchi and kokuinchi}} estate, but in {{ill|lt=1656|Q6982|WD=Q6982|1=1656}} ({{ill|lt=Joo|Q1145081|WD=Q1145081|1=Jōō (Edo period)}} 3) flooding of the Sakawa River washed away the shrine buildings. It was rebuilt in {{ill|lt=1868|Q7717|WD=Q7717|1=1868}} ({{ill|lt=Keio|Q776775|WD=Q776775|1=Keiō}} 4). It became a {{ill|lt=Gosha|Q134917316|WD=Q134917316|1=Gō-sha}} in {{ill|lt=1874|Q7761|WD=Q7761|1=1874}} ({{ill|lt=Meiji|Q3125096|WD=Q3125096|1=Meiji era}} 7), and a {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Prefectural Shrine|1=UNKNOWN}} in {{ill|lt=1941|Q5231|WD=Q5231|1=1941}} ({{ill|lt=Showa|Q327567|WD=Q327567|1=Shōwa era}} 16). Later, in {{ill|lt=1968|Q2644|WD=Q2644|1=1968}} (Showa 43), it was designated by the Kanagawa Prefectural Association of Shinto Shrines as a {{ill|lt=List of shrines in Kanagawa Prefecture#Shrines designated by Kanagawa Shrine Association|Q11588941|WD=Q11588941|lt=shrine visited by imperial envoy for offerings|1=Kanagawa Prefecture Shrine Office Designated Shrine}}, and in {{ill|lt=1971|Q2475|WD=Q2475|1=1971}} (Showa 46) it was designated a {{ill|lt=historic site|Q1081138|WD=Q1081138|1=Historic site}} by Matsuda Town. === Rituals === * {{ill|lt=Annual festival|Q11385469|WD=Q11385469|lt=Grand annual festival|1=Reisai}} (July 31) ** The mikoshi procession was designated a Matsuda Town {{ill|lt=intangible cultural property|Q2901957|WD=Q2901957|1=Intangible Cultural Property (Japan)}} in 1971 (Showa 46). ** In the past, the annual festival was held on the last day of June each year. === Precincts === === Precinct shrines === *Inari Shrine *Sorei Shrine *Kumano Shrine, Shinmei Shrine, Tenshajin File:寒田神社(摂末社-稲荷社).jpg|Inari Shrine File:寒田神社(摂末社-祖霊社).jpg|Sorei Shrine File:寒田神社(摂末社-熊野社・神明社・天社神).jpg|Kumano Shrine, Shinmei Shrine, Tenshajin === Cultural properties === *The oak at Samuta Shrine - selected among Kanagawa's 100 notable trees File:寒田神社-カシの木.jpg|Oak at Samuta Shrine File:寒田神社-親子狛犬.jpg|Parent-and-child komainu === Other === *{{ill|lt=John Herschel Glenn|Q182642|WD=Q182642|lt=John Glenn|1=John Glenn}}, the {{ill|lt=astronaut|Q11631|WD=Q11631|1=Astronaut}} who first succeeded in orbiting the {{ill|lt=Earth|Q2|WD=Q2|1=Earth}} for the {{ill|lt=United States|Q30|WD=Q30|1=United States}}, carried a Samuta Shrine prayer talisman during spaceflight and visited the shrine in thanks after returning to Earth. === Access === * 5-minute walk from {{ill|lt=Matsuda Station|Q5357522|WD=Q5357522|1=Matsuda Station}} on {{ill|lt=JR Tokai|Q513679|WD=Q513679|1=Central Japan Railway Company}} {{ill|lt=Gotemba Line|Q667927|WD=Q667927|1=Gotemba Line}}. * 7-minute walk from {{ill|lt=Shin-Matsuda Station|Q4419978|WD=Q4419978|1=Shin-Matsuda Station}} on {{ill|lt=Odakyu Electric Railway|Q83584|WD=Q83584|1=Odakyu Electric Railway}} {{ill|lt=Odakyu Odawara Line|Q135176|WD=Q135176|lt=Odawara Line|1=Odakyū Odawara Line}}. * About 10 minutes by car from {{ill|lt=Oi-Matsuda Interchange|Q10933470|WD=Q10933470|1=Oi-Matsuda interchange}} on the {{ill|lt=Tomei Expressway|Q1135694|WD=Q1135694|1=Tōmei Expressway}}. === Footnotes === {{脚注ヘルプ}} === Notes === Haiden
{{maplink2|frame=yes|plain=yes|type=point|zoom=13|marker=religious-shinto|frame-align=center|frame-width=250|coord={{coord|35|20|45|N|139|8|1|E}}}} |所在地={{ill|lt=Kanagawa Prefecture|Q127513|WD=Q127513|1=Kanagawa Prefecture}}{{ill|lt=Ashigarakami District|Q1202540|WD=Q1202540|1=Ashigarakami District, Kanagawa}}{{ill|lt=Matsuda|Q1202796|WD=Q1202796|1=Matsuda, Kanagawa}}Matsuda Soryo 1767 |位置={{ウィキ座標2段度分秒|35|20|45|N|139|8|1|E}} |祭神={{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Yamato Takeru-no-Mikoto|1=UNKNOWN}}
{{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Ototachibana-hime-no-Mikoto|1=UNKNOWN}}
{{ill|lt=Sugawara no Michizane|Q382005|WD=Q382005|1=Sugawara no Michizane}}
{{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Homutawake-no-Mikoto|1=UNKNOWN}} |社格={{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Shikinaisha|1=Shikinaisha|WD=Q134917286}} (small), {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Prefectural Shrine|1=UNKNOWN}} (former rank under {{ill|lt=Modern shrine ranking system|Q712534|WD=Q712534|1=Modern system of ranked Shinto shrines}}) |創建=Emperor Nintoku 3 (315) |例祭=July 31 |地図 = {{Location map|Japan Kanagawa|label=Samuta Shrine|position=bottom|width=200|mark=Shinto torii icon vermillion.svg|marksize=15|float=center|caption=}} }} '''Samuta Shrine''' (さむたじんじゃ) is a {{ill|lt=shrine|Q845945|WD=Q845945|1=Shinto shrine}} in Matsuda Soryo, {{ill|lt=Matsuda|Q1202796|WD=Q1202796|1=Matsuda, Kanagawa}}, {{ill|lt=Ashigarakami District|Q1202540|WD=Q1202540|1=Ashigarakami District, Kanagawa}}, {{ill|lt=Kanagawa Prefecture|Q127513|WD=Q127513|1=Kanagawa Prefecture}}. It is regarded as one of the thirteen Engishiki shrines (small shrine) of {{ill|lt=Sagami Province|Q1053732|WD=Q1053732|1=Sagami Province}} listed in the ''{{ill|lt=Engishiki Jinmyocho|Q11064932|WD=Q11064932|1=Engishiki Jinmyōchō}}'' of {{ill|lt=927|Q30470|WD=Q30470|1=927}} ({{ill|lt=Encho (era)|Q1327274|WD=Q1327274|lt=Encho|1=Enchō}} 5). Its precincts face the {{ill|lt=Sakawa River|Q11644295|WD=Q11644295|1=Sakawa River}}. === Enshrined deities === * {{ill|lt=Yamato Takeru|Q461258|WD=Q461258|lt=Yamato Takeru-no-Mikoto|1=Yamato Takeru}} * {{ill|lt=Ototachibana-hime|Q11487396|WD=Q11487396|lt=Ototachibana-hime-no-Mikoto|1=Tachibanahime-no-Mikoto}} * {{ill|lt=Sugawara no Michizane|Q382005|WD=Q382005|1=Sugawara no Michizane}} * {{ill|lt=Hachiman deity|Q261637|WD=Q261637|lt=Homutawake-no-Mikoto|1=Hachiman}} === History === Founded in the era of {{ill|lt=Emperor Nintoku|Q313119|WD=Q313119|1=Emperor Nintoku}}. It is mentioned in ancient {{ill|lt=fudoki|Q1194534|WD=Q1194534|1=Fudoki}}. It was formerly also called Sagamita Shrine, Sabuta Shrine, and Samuta Shrine. In the ''{{ill|lt=Engishiki Jinmyocho|Q11064932|WD=Q11064932|1=Engishiki Jinmyōchō}}'' it appears as "Sagami no kuni, Ashigami District, Samuta Shrine, small." In the Edo period it was also called Samuta Shrine Kanda Daimyojin{{efn|In ''{{ill|lt=Shinpen Sagami no Kuni Fudokikō|Q11503942|WD=Q11503942|1=Shinpen Sagami no Kuni Fudoki-kō}}'' it is written as Kanda Myojinsha{{sfn|新編相模国風土記稿 松田惣領 神田明神社}}.}}. According to shrine tradition, Yamato Takeru stayed here for a while during his eastern expedition. It is said that when he prayed here for success, he poured sake into the Sakawa River, and when he returned after the campaign, the fragrance of sake still remained, so the river came to be called Sakawa. A wooden bowl said to be from that event is preserved at the shrine. In {{ill|lt=1626|Q6831|WD=Q6831|1=1626}} ({{ill|lt=Kanei|Q1192913|WD=Q1192913|1=Kan'ei}} 3), {{ill|lt=Tokugawa Iemitsu|Q314481|WD=Q314481|1=Tokugawa Iemitsu}} donated a {{ill|lt=shuinchi / kokuinchi|Q11521520|WD=Q11521520|lt=shuinchi|1=shuinchi and kokuinchi}} estate, but in {{ill|lt=1656|Q6982|WD=Q6982|1=1656}} ({{ill|lt=Joo|Q1145081|WD=Q1145081|1=Jōō (Edo period)}} 3) flooding of the Sakawa River washed away the shrine buildings. It was rebuilt in {{ill|lt=1868|Q7717|WD=Q7717|1=1868}} ({{ill|lt=Keio|Q776775|WD=Q776775|1=Keiō}} 4). It became a {{ill|lt=Gosha|Q134917316|WD=Q134917316|1=Gō-sha}} in {{ill|lt=1874|Q7761|WD=Q7761|1=1874}} ({{ill|lt=Meiji|Q3125096|WD=Q3125096|1=Meiji era}} 7), and a {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Prefectural Shrine|1=UNKNOWN}} in {{ill|lt=1941|Q5231|WD=Q5231|1=1941}} ({{ill|lt=Showa|Q327567|WD=Q327567|1=Shōwa era}} 16). Later, in {{ill|lt=1968|Q2644|WD=Q2644|1=1968}} (Showa 43), it was designated by the Kanagawa Prefectural Association of Shinto Shrines as a {{ill|lt=List of shrines in Kanagawa Prefecture#Shrines designated by Kanagawa Shrine Association|Q11588941|WD=Q11588941|lt=shrine visited by imperial envoy for offerings|1=Kanagawa Prefecture Shrine Office Designated Shrine}}, and in {{ill|lt=1971|Q2475|WD=Q2475|1=1971}} (Showa 46) it was designated a {{ill|lt=historic site|Q1081138|WD=Q1081138|1=Historic site}} by Matsuda Town. === Rituals === * {{ill|lt=Annual festival|Q11385469|WD=Q11385469|lt=Grand annual festival|1=Reisai}} (July 31) ** The mikoshi procession was designated a Matsuda Town {{ill|lt=intangible cultural property|Q2901957|WD=Q2901957|1=Intangible Cultural Property (Japan)}} in 1971 (Showa 46). ** In the past, the annual festival was held on the last day of June each year. === Precincts === === Precinct shrines === *Inari Shrine *Sorei Shrine *Kumano Shrine, Shinmei Shrine, Tenshajin File:寒田神社(摂末社-稲荷社).jpg|Inari Shrine File:寒田神社(摂末社-祖霊社).jpg|Sorei Shrine File:寒田神社(摂末社-熊野社・神明社・天社神).jpg|Kumano Shrine, Shinmei Shrine, Tenshajin === Cultural properties === *The oak at Samuta Shrine - selected among Kanagawa's 100 notable trees File:寒田神社-カシの木.jpg|Oak at Samuta Shrine File:寒田神社-親子狛犬.jpg|Parent-and-child komainu === Other === *{{ill|lt=John Herschel Glenn|Q182642|WD=Q182642|lt=John Glenn|1=John Glenn}}, the {{ill|lt=astronaut|Q11631|WD=Q11631|1=Astronaut}} who first succeeded in orbiting the {{ill|lt=Earth|Q2|WD=Q2|1=Earth}} for the {{ill|lt=United States|Q30|WD=Q30|1=United States}}, carried a Samuta Shrine prayer talisman during spaceflight and visited the shrine in thanks after returning to Earth. === Access === * 5-minute walk from {{ill|lt=Matsuda Station|Q5357522|WD=Q5357522|1=Matsuda Station}} on {{ill|lt=JR Tokai|Q513679|WD=Q513679|1=Central Japan Railway Company}} {{ill|lt=Gotemba Line|Q667927|WD=Q667927|1=Gotemba Line}}. * 7-minute walk from {{ill|lt=Shin-Matsuda Station|Q4419978|WD=Q4419978|1=Shin-Matsuda Station}} on {{ill|lt=Odakyu Electric Railway|Q83584|WD=Q83584|1=Odakyu Electric Railway}} {{ill|lt=Odakyu Odawara Line|Q135176|WD=Q135176|lt=Odawara Line|1=Odakyū Odawara Line}}. * About 10 minutes by car from {{ill|lt=Oi-Matsuda Interchange|Q10933470|WD=Q10933470|1=Oi-Matsuda interchange}} on the {{ill|lt=Tomei Expressway|Q1135694|WD=Q1135694|1=Tōmei Expressway}}. === Footnotes === {{脚注ヘルプ}} === Notes === {{maplink2|frame=yes|plain=yes|type=point|zoom=13|marker=religious-shinto|frame-align=center|frame-width=250|coord={{coord|35|20|45|N|139|8|1|E}}}} |所在地={{ill|lt=Kanagawa Prefecture|Q127513|WD=Q127513|1=Kanagawa Prefecture}}{{ill|lt=Ashigarakami District|Q1202540|WD=Q1202540|1=Ashigarakami District, Kanagawa}}{{ill|lt=Matsuda|Q1202796|WD=Q1202796|1=Matsuda, Kanagawa}}Matsuda Soryo 1767 |位置={{ウィキ座標2段度分秒|35|20|45|N|139|8|1|E}} |祭神={{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Yamato Takeru-no-Mikoto|1=UNKNOWN}}
{{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Ototachibana-hime-no-Mikoto|1=UNKNOWN}}
{{ill|lt=Sugawara no Michizane|Q382005|WD=Q382005|1=Sugawara no Michizane}}
{{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Homutawake-no-Mikoto|1=UNKNOWN}} |社格={{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Shikinaisha|1=Shikinaisha|WD=Q134917286}} (small), {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Prefectural Shrine|1=UNKNOWN}} (former rank under {{ill|lt=Modern shrine ranking system|Q712534|WD=Q712534|1=Modern system of ranked Shinto shrines}}) |創建=Emperor Nintoku 3 (315) |例祭=July 31 |地図 = {{Location map|Japan Kanagawa|label=Samuta Shrine|position=bottom|width=200|mark=Shinto torii icon vermillion.svg|marksize=15|float=center|caption=}} }} '''Samuta Shrine''' (さむたじんじゃ) is a {{ill|lt=shrine|Q845945|WD=Q845945|1=Shinto shrine}} in Matsuda Soryo, {{ill|lt=Matsuda|Q1202796|WD=Q1202796|1=Matsuda, Kanagawa}}, {{ill|lt=Ashigarakami District|Q1202540|WD=Q1202540|1=Ashigarakami District, Kanagawa}}, {{ill|lt=Kanagawa Prefecture|Q127513|WD=Q127513|1=Kanagawa Prefecture}}. It is regarded as one of the thirteen Engishiki shrines (small shrine) of {{ill|lt=Sagami Province|Q1053732|WD=Q1053732|1=Sagami Province}} listed in the ''{{ill|lt=Engishiki Jinmyocho|Q11064932|WD=Q11064932|1=Engishiki Jinmyōchō}}'' of {{ill|lt=927|Q30470|WD=Q30470|1=927}} ({{ill|lt=Encho (era)|Q1327274|WD=Q1327274|lt=Encho|1=Enchō}} 5). Its precincts face the {{ill|lt=Sakawa River|Q11644295|WD=Q11644295|1=Sakawa River}}. === Enshrined deities === * {{ill|lt=Yamato Takeru|Q461258|WD=Q461258|lt=Yamato Takeru-no-Mikoto|1=Yamato Takeru}} * {{ill|lt=Ototachibana-hime|Q11487396|WD=Q11487396|lt=Ototachibana-hime-no-Mikoto|1=Tachibanahime-no-Mikoto}} * {{ill|lt=Sugawara no Michizane|Q382005|WD=Q382005|1=Sugawara no Michizane}} * {{ill|lt=Hachiman deity|Q261637|WD=Q261637|lt=Homutawake-no-Mikoto|1=Hachiman}} === History === Founded in the era of {{ill|lt=Emperor Nintoku|Q313119|WD=Q313119|1=Emperor Nintoku}}. It is mentioned in ancient {{ill|lt=fudoki|Q1194534|WD=Q1194534|1=Fudoki}}. It was formerly also called Sagamita Shrine, Sabuta Shrine, and Samuta Shrine. In the ''{{ill|lt=Engishiki Jinmyocho|Q11064932|WD=Q11064932|1=Engishiki Jinmyōchō}}'' it appears as "Sagami no kuni, Ashigami District, Samuta Shrine, small." In the Edo period it was also called Samuta Shrine Kanda Daimyojin{{efn|In ''{{ill|lt=Shinpen Sagami no Kuni Fudokikō|Q11503942|WD=Q11503942|1=Shinpen Sagami no Kuni Fudoki-kō}}'' it is written as Kanda Myojinsha{{sfn|新編相模国風土記稿 松田惣領 神田明神社}}.}}. According to shrine tradition, Yamato Takeru stayed here for a while during his eastern expedition. It is said that when he prayed here for success, he poured sake into the Sakawa River, and when he returned after the campaign, the fragrance of sake still remained, so the river came to be called Sakawa. A wooden bowl said to be from that event is preserved at the shrine. In {{ill|lt=1626|Q6831|WD=Q6831|1=1626}} ({{ill|lt=Kanei|Q1192913|WD=Q1192913|1=Kan'ei}} 3), {{ill|lt=Tokugawa Iemitsu|Q314481|WD=Q314481|1=Tokugawa Iemitsu}} donated a {{ill|lt=shuinchi / kokuinchi|Q11521520|WD=Q11521520|lt=shuinchi|1=shuinchi and kokuinchi}} estate, but in {{ill|lt=1656|Q6982|WD=Q6982|1=1656}} ({{ill|lt=Joo|Q1145081|WD=Q1145081|1=Jōō (Edo period)}} 3) flooding of the Sakawa River washed away the shrine buildings. It was rebuilt in {{ill|lt=1868|Q7717|WD=Q7717|1=1868}} ({{ill|lt=Keio|Q776775|WD=Q776775|1=Keiō}} 4). It became a {{ill|lt=Gosha|Q134917316|WD=Q134917316|1=Gō-sha}} in {{ill|lt=1874|Q7761|WD=Q7761|1=1874}} ({{ill|lt=Meiji|Q3125096|WD=Q3125096|1=Meiji era}} 7), and a {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Prefectural Shrine|1=UNKNOWN}} in {{ill|lt=1941|Q5231|WD=Q5231|1=1941}} ({{ill|lt=Showa|Q327567|WD=Q327567|1=Shōwa era}} 16). Later, in {{ill|lt=1968|Q2644|WD=Q2644|1=1968}} (Showa 43), it was designated by the Kanagawa Prefectural Association of Shinto Shrines as a {{ill|lt=List of shrines in Kanagawa Prefecture#Shrines designated by Kanagawa Shrine Association|Q11588941|WD=Q11588941|lt=shrine visited by imperial envoy for offerings|1=Kanagawa Prefecture Shrine Office Designated Shrine}}, and in {{ill|lt=1971|Q2475|WD=Q2475|1=1971}} (Showa 46) it was designated a {{ill|lt=historic site|Q1081138|WD=Q1081138|1=Historic site}} by Matsuda Town. === Rituals === * {{ill|lt=Annual festival|Q11385469|WD=Q11385469|lt=Grand annual festival|1=Reisai}} (July 31) ** The mikoshi procession was designated a Matsuda Town {{ill|lt=intangible cultural property|Q2901957|WD=Q2901957|1=Intangible Cultural Property (Japan)}} in 1971 (Showa 46). ** In the past, the annual festival was held on the last day of June each year. === Precincts === === succeeded in orbiting the {{ill|lt=Earth|Q2|WD=Q2|1=Earth}} for the {{ill|lt=United States|Q30|WD=Q30|1=United States}}, carried a Samuta Shrine prayer talisman during spaceflight and visited the
